text: Ventromesh Rectopaxi has gained popularity in the treatment of full thickness rectal prolapse in view of reported lower recurrence rates and lesser post-operative constipation. This is a forty-five-year Chinese female with significant obstetric history of four normal vaginal deliveries with episiotomies. She presented with a history of obstructed defecation and a Wexner constipation score of nineteen. On examination, she has no obvious prolapse, an anterior rectocele and a normal anal tone. This is an image of a defecating proctogram, which shows an abnormal descent of her anal rectal junction, an intra-rectal interception and an anterior rectocele.

The patient is positioned in a modified Lloyd-Davies position with a bladder catheter inserted. The surgical setup includes a ten millimeter subarm ligoport and two five millimeter ports on each side of the abdomen. The surgeon retracts the uterus to visualize the area of intrarectal interception and places transabdominal proline sutures to retract the bulky uterus.

The surgeon makes a peritoneal incision over the sacral promontory, extending it distally in an inverted J fashion along the right side of the rectum and over the deepest part of the pouch of Douglas. An anterior-only rectal mobilization is performed, carefully avoiding the lateral neurovascular pedicles.

The surgeon performs posterior dissection limited to the sacral promontory. A malleable retractor is inserted into the vagina to delineate the vaginal wall, aiding in the dissection. The rectovaginal septum is incised and dissected down to the pelvic floor, reaching approximately two to three centimeters above the dentate line.

The surgeon trims a proline mesh into an L-shaped configuration and secures it onto the pelvic floor muscles on either side of the upper anal canal using tackers. The mesh is carefully positioned to avoid direct attachment to the rectal wall, preventing potential mesh infection and erosion.
The surgeon anchors the mesh to the sacral promontory using Etibon II-O sutures, securing it in place as part of the rectopexy procedure.
The surgeon closes the peritoneum over the mesh using V-lock sutures, completing the procedure with a view of the final surgical site.
The total laparoscopic time was a hundred and twenty minutes. The patient went home well on post-op day one.

The Association of Colour Proctology of Great Britain and Ireland recommends against the use of prosthetic meshes because of its risk of erosion and fissilation. A recent systemic review found increased mesh erosion rates with synthetic meshes as compared to biologic meshes. During our initial experience, proline meshes were used instead of biologic meshes as they were not available. However, in the past one year, we have replaced proline meshes with biologic meshes as they have been made available. Suture rectopexy is not preferred due to poor reported outcomes.
Thank you for your kind attention.
